intro: A quieter Sunday after Kimi K3's shockwaves, but the fallout keeps surfacing: Washington moves to control who gets access to frontier AI models, Google's Gemini 3.5 Pro slips again over coding shortfalls, and bitcoin's Coinbase premium hits a record losing streak as Congress argues over crypto rules.

items.headline: Google delays Gemini 3.5 Pro again after it misses internal coding targets
items.summary: Google's flagship Gemini 3.5 Pro remains months behind its original schedule after the model fell short of internal expectations on coding and complex reasoning tasks; the company reset its training data in June to address the gap but results were reportedly still disappointing.
items.why: Google is now the one flagship lab visibly behind on coding capability, just as Anthropic, OpenAI and open-weight Chinese labs all ship competitive coding models.

items.headline: VS Code 1.129 ships a dedicated agent host and a new Agents editor panel
items.summary: The July release of Visual Studio Code adds a dedicated background process for running agent sessions that multiple windows can connect to, a new experimental editor panel for reviewing agent-generated diffs, the ability to run terminal commands from chat with a "!" prefix, and a preview of a redesigned workbench UI.
items.why: It's Microsoft further restructuring the editor itself around long-running, multi-window agent sessions rather than single inline suggestions.

items.headline: Indian vibe-coding startup Emergent becomes a unicorn a year after launch
items.summary: Emergent raised a $130 million Series C led by Creaegis at a $1.5 billion valuation, a five-fold jump in six months; the AI-agent app-building platform now has an annualized run rate of $120 million and more than 200,000 paying customers.
items.why: It's one of the fastest unicorn runs of the year, and a sign investor appetite for AI app-building tools hasn't cooled despite a crowded field.

items.headline: The White House starts dictating who gets access to frontier AI models
items.summary: The Trump administration has begun directly deciding which companies and government entities can access the most capable models from Anthropic and OpenAI, a decision that until now sat with the labs themselves; it follows a June executive order asking AI companies to give the government early access to models for testing.
items.why: It's a real shift in leverage from the AI labs to the federal government over who can use the most powerful models, arriving the same week a Chinese open-weight model narrowed the capability gap.

items.headline: Thinking Machines ships Inkling, its first open-weight model
items.summary: Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab released Inkling, a 975-billion-parameter mixture-of-experts model (41 billion active parameters) that reasons over text, images and audio with a 1-million-token context window, plus a smaller preview variant called Inkling-Small.
items.why: It's the company's first public model release and adds another credible open-weight frontier option alongside this month's Chinese releases.

items.headline: OpenAI says automated red-teaming model GPT-Red keeps cutting jailbreak success rates
items.summary: OpenAI detailed GPT-Red, an internal-only automated red-teaming model trained at large compute scale that generates attacks during training to harden production models; the company says GPT-5.6 Sol now fails on only 0.05% of GPT-Red's direct prompt-injection attempts.
items.why: It's a concrete example of using models to find and close their own successors' safety gaps before release, rather than relying only on human red-teamers.

items.headline: Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday fixes a record 570 flaws, including 3 zero-days
items.summary: Microsoft's largest Patch Tuesday ever addressed 570 vulnerabilities, 59 of them critical, including three zero-days (two already exploited in the wild); Microsoft credited an internal AI-driven vulnerability-scanning system with helping surface the unusually high volume.
items.why: Two of the zero-days are already under active attack, so unpatched Windows and SharePoint deployments are an immediate risk.

items.headline: GitHub's CodeQL 2.26 adds Kotlin 2.4 support and AI prompt-injection detection
items.summary: GitHub shipped CodeQL 2.26.0 with support for Kotlin 2.4.0, a new JavaScript/TypeScript query that flags system-prompt injection vulnerabilities, and accuracy improvements across several languages; the update rolls out automatically to GitHub code scanning.
items.why: It's one of the first mainstream static-analysis checks aimed specifically at prompt-injection bugs in AI-integrated code.

items.headline: npm v12 turns off install scripts and remote/git dependencies by default
items.summary: npm v12, now the default registry client, no longer runs preinstall, install or postinstall scripts (including implicit native builds) or resolves git and remote-URL dependencies unless a project explicitly allows them, closing off a technique used in numerous supply-chain attacks.
items.why: Teams that haven't run `npm approve-scripts` yet will see builds start failing until they explicitly allowlist the scripts they trust.

items.headline: Coinbase's bitcoin premium stays negative for a record 60 straight days
items.summary: Bitcoin fell below $63,000 on July 17 as the Coinbase Bitcoin Premium Index extended its negative streak to 60 consecutive days, the longest on record and well past the prior 40-day streak from January-February, a signal analysts read as persistently weak U.S. institutional demand.
items.why: A record-long negative premium points to structurally soft U.S. buying pressure even as bitcoin's price has partly recovered from June's lows.

items.headline: House CLARITY Act hearing in New York pressures Senate ahead of August recess
items.summary: The House Financial Services Committee held a field hearing at Federal Hall on July 17 pushing the Senate to pass the CLARITY Act, the bill that would split digital-asset oversight between the SEC and CFTC; the Senate still lacks the seven to nine Democratic votes needed to clear a filibuster, with disputes over insider-trading rules and stablecoin yield unresolved.
items.why: This is the last realistic window for crypto market-structure legislation before Congress leaves for August recess.
